runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in the U.S. aggregate private sector.
runs.reasoningSummary: Modern empirical estimates often find values significantly below the Cobb-Douglas benchmark of 1.0, though recent debates concerning the declining labor share have suggested values might be above unity if driven by capital-augmenting technological change. My distribution leans toward gross complementarity (< 1) while acknowledging uncertainty introduced by different estimation methods and timeframes.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in the U.S. aggregate private sector.",
  "point_estimate": 0.85,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.4,
    "p25": 0.65,
    "p50": 0.85,
    "p75": 1.1,
    "p95": 1.4
  },
  "citations": [
    "Antras (2004)",
    "Chirinko (2008)",
    "Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014)"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Modern empirical estimates often find values significantly below the Cobb-Douglas benchmark of 1.0, though recent debates concerning the declining labor share have suggested values might be above unity if driven by capital-augmenting technological change. My distribution leans toward gross complementarity (< 1) while acknowledging uncertainty introduced by different estimation methods and timeframes."
}

runs.interpretation: Long-run aggregate elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in the U.S. private sector within a CES framework.
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical evidence generally suggests that the elasticity of substitution is below unity (the Cobb-Douglas case), with time-series and panel data typically yielding estimates between 0.4 and 0.8. runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in the aggregate private-sector production of the United States.
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical literature consistently challenges the Cobb-Douglas assumption of unit elasticity. Time-series estimates often yield lower values (0.4-0.6), while cross-sectional and industry-level aggregations suggest values closer to 1. Long-run adjustments and capital-augmenting technical change support an aggregate estimate slightly below unity.

  "interpretation": "Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in the aggregate private-sector production of the United States.",

    "p25": 0.65,
    "p50": 0.85,
    "p75": 1.0,

    "Chirinko (2008)",
    "Oberfield and Raval (2021)"

Time-series estimates often yield lower values (0.4-0.6), while cross-sectional and industry-level aggregations suggest values closer to 1. Long-run adjustments and capital-augmenting technical change support an aggregate estimate slightly below unity."
}

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in aggregate U.S. private-sector production.
runs.reasoningSummary: While the Cobb-Douglas assumption of unit elasticity is common, empirical estimates using time-series and panel data often find values significantly below 1. I weight the evidence toward gross complementarity (0.6–0.9), but include a tail above 1 to account for recent literature suggesting capital-skill complementarity or automation-driven substitution.

    "Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014)"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "While the Cobb-Douglas assumption of unit elasticity is a standard benchmark, empirical evidence often suggests values below 1.0 (biased toward 0.5-0.8) for aggregate production, though recent debates regarding the decline of the labor share have led some researchers to argue for values slightly above 1.0."
}
